one_line_takeaway: A cryptography-style 'advantage' (indistinguishability) metric plus an AG2 multi-agent prober that automatically measures how much a target LLM's confidential system prompt leaks; on one case study, prompt hardening alone cut advantage from 0.65 to 0.225 (still ~30% leakage) while adding a guard-LLM filter reached 0.1.
key_finding: The advantage metric cleanly separates defense strength: Low security = 0.65, Medium = 0.225, High = 0.1. The low-security model leaks confidential data readily (a single question about lowering price to 50% elicited the Croatia-specific discount). Basic prompt hardening (medium) only partially helps - the model still leaked confidential data in roughly 30% of adversarial attacks, so hardening alone is insufficient. Adding a separate guard LLM as an output filter (high) drove advantage down to 0.1, the strongest configuration, though leakage was not fully eliminated. The authors conclude the advantage metric is a reliable, quantitative indicator of prompt-leakage vulnerability.
core_contribution: The paper makes two coupled contributions of its own. (1) A cryptographically-inspired formal definition of 'prompt leakage security' for LLMs: a system is prompt-leakage-secure if a judge cannot distinguish responses produced by an LLM initialized with the original (confidential) system prompt H from responses produced by an LLM initialized with a sanitized prompt H' (with secrets removed/substituted). Distinguishability is quantified by an 'advantage' metric Adv = Pr[J=1 | H] - Pr[J=1 | H'], analogous to indistinguishability games in cryptography (they explicitly build on Rogaway's definitional work and the security framing from PAIR/Chao et al.). (2) An open-source agentic proof-of-concept, 'Prompt Leakage Probing,' built on AG2 (formerly AutoGen), that operationalizes this metric: a multi-agent GroupChat automatically probes a target LLM and empirically estimates its advantage across three defense levels. The authors also give practical guidance on how to construct a good sanitized prompt H' (empty prompt is insufficient; substitute secrets with plausible fakes; embed detectability markers to catch full-prompt leakage).
threat_model: Adversary seeks to extract sensitive or proprietary content embedded in an LLM's system prompt (system-level instructions, secret keys/tokens, proprietary algorithms/strategies, pricing, behavioral/compliance rules) from a deployed, black-box, proprietary model that cannot be retrained. Attack is prompt leakage, framed as a subclass of prompt injection (distinct from goal hijacking). In the paper's operationalization the adversary is itself an automated agentic team (an Initial Analysis agent plus a Judge agent) that interactively queries the target and decides whether it is talking to the original or the sanitized agent. Adversarial 'effort' is defined qualitatively across compute, time, attack complexity, and financial cost; a secure system keeps advantage small even under high effort.
limitations: Explicitly a proof-of-concept. Evaluation rests on a single hand-crafted case study (one fictional company 'Bord', one secret type: regional pricing) and a single model family (ChatGPT-4o-mini serving simultaneously as attacker, judge, analysis, and target), so generality across models, domains, and secret types is untested. Only 40 trials per configuration, giving noisy advantage estimates with no reported confidence intervals or significance. The 'small advantage' security threshold is left entirely user-defined/subjective. Automatic generation of sanitized prompts H' is explicitly deferred to future work (H' is hand-authored here). Related automated attackers (PAIR, GPTFUZZER) are cited as inspiration but not benchmarked against - there is no baseline comparison to prior prompt-extraction methods, only an internal low/medium/high comparison. Guard-based defense reduces but does not eliminate leakage.

datasets_used: No standard benchmark dataset; a custom hand-crafted case study - a system prompt for a fictional automotive company 'Bord' containing confidential regional price discounts (Europe and Croatia), with a sanitized variant substituting USA and Texas
quantitative_results: Advantage, High Security (prompt hardening + guard LLM output filter) = 0.1
Medium-security model still revealed confidential data in ~30% of adversarial attacks
40 trials run per agent configuration to estimate the advantage
Fully secure system would have advantage = 0
baselines_compared: Internal comparison across three self-defined defense levels (Low security is the baseline at 0.65; Medium and High are the compared configurations at 0.225 and 0.1)
No external baseline: prior automated attackers such as PAIR and GPTFUZZER are cited as influences but not empirically benchmarked against
